CLARITY.justification: The paper is well organized and follows a clear structure (Introduction → Background → Experiments → Results → Discussion → Appendices), and the main contributions (predicting active medication classes from billing-code sequences and comparing RNNs to feed-forward models) are stated early in the Introduction. Notation and mathematical measures are generally well defined (e.g. d={E,T,y}, micro-AUC and label-ranking loss equations in Section 3.1–3.4). Figures/tables and case examples are effective and informative where used (detailed case summaries and Appendix A hyperparameters), but several references to Table 1 and figures are not present in the provided text and some visual material may be missing. Presentation issues include occasional duplicated/repeated sentences and minor grammatical/formatting errors (notably in the closing paragraphs), which reduce polish but do not obscure the paper’s argument.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper applies well-known sequence models (LSTM/GRU and embeddings) to a specific practical formulation—predicting a patient’s active medication therapeutic classes from a sequence of ICD-9 billing codes—and directly compares recurrent vs. similarly configured feed‑forward models. Prior work cited already uses RNNs for related medical prediction tasks (Lipton et al.; Choi et al.) and collaborative-filter approaches have been used to predict missing medications, so the methods themselves are not new. The novelty lies in the particular learning problem (predicting all active meds from billing-code sequences), the emphasis on isolating the value of temporal recurrence, and the much larger dataset (≈3.3M instances, 610k patients), which makes this an incremental but notable empirical contribution rather than a fundamentally new method.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's central claims (RNNs—especially a GRU—can predict active medication classes from ICD-9 code sequences and outperform a similar feed‑forward net and random forest) are supported by large-scale experiments (3.3M instances, patient‑level train/val/test split) and standard multi‑label metrics (Section 3.1, 3.4) including reported figures (micro‑AUC ≈0.93, LR≈0.076). Mathematical material is limited to metric definitions and is correct. Minor technical issues remain: the feed‑forward baseline’s exact handling of the sequence input is under‑specified (how the sequence is collapsed/represented), the random‑forest baseline required a sharded ensemble (3.2.3) which may be suboptimal, no statistical significance/confidence intervals are reported, the embedding evaluation used a single clinician rater, and the paper asserts an equivalence between 1−AUC and label‑ranking loss for an instance without rigorous justification (Results). These do not invalidate the main conclusions but leave room for clarification and additional analysis.
